note_data.title: Processor fundamentals
keyPoints: The **CPU** executes instructions by following the fetch-decode-execute cycle continuously
**Registers** are high-speed storage locations within the CPU that temporarily store data and instructions during processing
The **Accumulator (ACC)** stores the results of arithmetic and logical operations performed by the ALU
The **Program Counter (PC)** stores the address of the next instruction to be executed
The **Memory Address Register (MAR)** stores the address of the memory location currently being accessed
The **Memory Data Register (MDR)** stores data that is being transferred to or from memory
The **Current Instruction Register (CIR)** stores the instruction that is currently being decoded and executed
The **address bus** carries memory addresses from the CPU to memory and is unidirectional
The **data bus** carries data between the CPU, memory and I/O devices and is bidirectional
The **control bus** carries control signals such as read/write signals and clock signals

definitions.definition: Central Processing Unit that executes instructions by following the fetch-decode-execute cycle
High-speed storage location within the CPU that temporarily stores data and instructions during processing
Set of parallel wires that carries data, addresses or control signals between components of a computer system
Complete set of machine code instructions that a particular CPU can understand and execute
Low-level programming language that uses mnemonics to represent machine code instructions

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give characteristics or features. Use 'The CPU...' or 'The register...'
Give similarities and differences between two components

modelAnswer.question: Explain the role of the Program Counter (PC) and Memory Address Register (MAR) during the fetch phase of the fetch-decode-execute cycle.
modelAnswer.answer: The Program Counter stores the address of the next instruction to be executed. During the fetch phase, the contents of the PC are copied to the Memory Address Register. The MAR then stores the address of the memory location that contains the instruction to be fetched. The PC is then incremented to point to the next instruction.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: PC stores address of next instruction
Mark 2: PC contents copied to MAR
Mark 3: MAR stores address of instruction location
Mark 4: PC incremented to next instruction

commonMistakes: Never say 'RAM stores instructions' — always say 'memory stores instructions and the CPU fetches them'
Students often confuse MAR and MDR — MAR stores addresses, MDR stores actual data
Don't say 'the bus transfers data' — say 'the data bus carries data between components'
Never describe registers as 'memory' — they are high-speed storage locations within the CPU
Students forget that the address bus is unidirectional while data bus is bidirectional
examinerTips: This topic appears in almost every Paper 2 exam - focus on register functions
Always specify which bus (address/data/control) when describing data transfer
Learn the exact sequence of the fetch-decode-execute cycle
Bus width questions often ask about relationship between address bus width and addressable memory
formulas: Addressable memory locations = 2^n (where n = number of address bus lines)
Data transfer rate = bus width × clock speed
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: CPU architecture diagram showing ALU, Control Unit, registers (PC, MAR, MDR, CIR, ACC), and the three buses (address, data, control) connecting to memory and I/O devices
